context:
Vibrations and waves are everywhere. If you take any system and disturb it from a stable equilibrium, the resultant motion will be waves and vibrations. Think of a guitar string—pluck the string, and it vibrates. The sound waves generated make their way to our ears, and we hear the string’s sound. Our eyes see what’s happening because they receive the electromagnetic waves of the light reflected from the guitar string, so that we can recognize the beautiful sinusoidal waves on the string. In fact, without vibrations and waves, we could not recognize the universe around us at all!

The amazing thing is that we can describe many fascinating phenomena arising from very different physical systems with mathematics. This course will provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. You will learn that waves come from many interconnected (coupled) objects when they are vibrating together. We will discuss many of these phenomena, along with related topics, including mechanical vibrations and waves, sound waves, electromagnetic waves, optics, and gravitational waves.

Syllabus:
8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves

Prerequisites:
18.02 Multivariable Calculus and 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism.

This course is designed for students who are already familiar with the concepts taught in 8.01 Classical Mechanics as well as 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism. We also rely on fundamental mathematics principles taught in 18.01 Single Variable Calculus and 18.02 Multivariable Calculus, as well as some aspects of 18.06 Linear Algebra.

Course Overview:
What are vibrations? Many physical systems vibrate or oscillate when something disrupts their equilibrium. Think of what happens when you pull a mass attached to a spring or push a pendulum in a certain direction. The mass bounces up and down. The pendulum swings back and forth. But even these simple phenomena can respond in complicated and counterintuitive ways when subjected to forces known in physics as damping and driving. Combining several pendulums or springs can lead to even more unexpected motions. In this course you will see how physics can describe these motions mathematically.

Extended physical systems that have been made to vibrate, like a string on a guitar, cannot return to their state of equilibrium without exerting forces on the area around them. These forces then lead to the phenomenon of waves, disturbances that propagate through a medium. The vibrating guitar string causes a sound wave to propagate through the medium of the air.

Among the most fascinating phenomena examined in the course are electromagnetic (EM) waves. EM waves have many properties similar to the waves we experience in everyday life, but the underlying physics of EM waves is profoundly different. Instead of a local disturbance exerting a force on adjoining regions, changing magnetic fields create electric fields (as quantified by Faraday's Law), and changing electric fields create magnetic fields (as quantified by Maxwell's extension of Ampere's Law).

Course Goals:
This course has two complementary goals. The first is to provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. This will allow you to gain a deeper appreciation for the true nature and beauty of phenomena like music and rainbows, which all of us observe or experience every day.

The second goal is to provide you with the skill of using a broad range of techniques that can greatly simplify the analysis and solution of complex systems. These techniques include complex numbers, combinations of oscillatory and exponentially decaying functions, resonance, normal modes, Cramer's rule for solving several equations in several variables, boundary conditions, general wave equations, Fourier decomposition, dispersive and non-dispersive media, phase and group velocities, sound cavities and wave guides, polarization, Doppler effect, reflection and refraction, Fresnel's equation for transmission and reflection, total internal reflection, constructive and destructive interference, and diffraction.

Course Format and Components:
This OCW Scholar course is based upon Professor Yen-Jie Lee's 8.03 Physics III: Vibrations and Waves course as taught on the MIT campus. It includes a complete set of 24 video lectures recorded at MIT in the Fall of 2016. Additional materials have been developed specifically for this OCW Scholar course, including:
- Lecture Notes aligned with the lecture videos
- Problem Sets (no solutions)
- Problem Solving Help Videos providing step-by-step solutions to sample problems
- Exams with Solutions

MIT students spend about 150-200 hours learning Vibrations and Waves in the on-campus version of this course. That number comes from a combination of attending lectures and recitations, and studying independently. It's difficult to estimate how long it will take you to complete all of the modules in this particular course, but you can probably expect to spend 3 to 4 hours on practice problems, readings and assessment for each hour of lecture video you watch.

Required Textbook:
Georgi, Howard. The Physics of Waves. Benjamin Cummings, 1992. ISBN: 9780136656210. Download the book (PDF - 5.5MB) (Courtesy of Howard Georgi. Used with permission.)

Suggested References:
French, A. P. Vibrations and Waves. New York: W.W. Norton, 1980. ISBN: 9780393099362.

Bekefi, George, and Alan H. Barrett. Electromagnetic Vibrations, Waves, and Radiation. MIT Press, 1977. ISBN: 9780262520478. [Preview with Google Books]
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 1

8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Fall 2016
Homework 1


Problem 1.1 (20 pts)

For the mass and spring discussed during the lecture (Howard Georgi Eq. (1.1)–(1.8)), suppose that the system is hung vertically in the earth's gravitational field, with the top of the spring held fixed. Show that the frequency for vertical oscillations is given by Eq. (1.5). Explain why gravity has no effect on the angular frequency.


Problem 1.2 (20 pts)

In each of the following three cases, the motion of a particle is specified by a complex representation of its displacement, velocity, or acceleration. For each case find a real representation for all three quantities — displacement, velocity, and acceleration — in the form
    η(t) = c cos ωt + d sin ωt
where c and d are real, time-independent quantities. A, B, C, and τ are real quantities. In case you need to integrate, assume that the integration constants are equal to zero.

(a) X(t) = A·e^(−i(ωt − π/2))
(b) Ẋ(t) = Biωτ·e^(−i(ωt + π/6))
(c) Ẍ(t) = C·(1/(1 + iωτ))·e^(−iωt)

In each of the following cases the motion of a particle is specified by a real representation of its displacement, velocity, or acceleration. For each case find the complex representation for all three quantities — displacement, velocity, and acceleration — in the form
    Q(t) = Q·e^(−i(ωt + φ))
where Q and φ are real, time-independent quantities. α, β, γ, and δ are real and positive quantities.

(d) x(t) = α cos ωt − β sin ωt
(e) ẋ(t) = −γ sin(ωt + π/3)
(f) ẍ(t) = δ cos(ωt + π/6)


Problem 1.3 (20 pts)

A block of mass M slides without friction between two springs of spring constants K and 2K as shown in Figure 1. The system is constrained to move only along the axis of the springs.

[Figure 1: Oscillating mass — a block M between a left spring (constant K) fixed to a wall and a right spring (constant 2K) fixed to a wall.]

(a) Calculate the angular frequency of oscillations.
(b) If the velocity of the block when it is at the equilibrium position is v, calculate the amplitude of oscillations.
(c) Write an expression for the position of the mass as a real function, x(t) (with the initial condition from (b)).
(d) Write an expression for the position of the mass as a complex function, z(t), in the irreducible form (with the initial condition from (b)).


Problem 1.4 (20 pts)

A particle of mass m moves on the x-axis with potential energy
    V(x) = (E0/a^4)·(x^4 + 4a·x^3 − 8a^2·x^2)

(a) Find the positions at which the particle is in stable equilibrium.
(b) Find the angular frequency of small oscillations about each stable equilibrium position.
(c) What do you mean by small oscillations? Be quantitative and give a separate answer for each point of stable equilibrium.


Problem 1.5 (20 pts)

Consider a simple pendulum consisting of a point-like mass m attached to a massless string of length L hanging from a fixed support and constrained to move in a vertical plane (see Figure 2). Assume gravitational acceleration to be g.

[Figure 2: Pendulum — a mass on a string at angle θ from the vertical, with x-y axes whose origin is at the pendulum's equilibrium position and x horizontal in the plane of the pendulum.]

(a) Parametrize the motion of the pendulum in terms of the angle θ, its deviation from the vertical. Find the exact equation of motion (τ = Iα) for the pendulum as a function of θ.
(b) Assume that the angle θ is small and find the approximate simple harmonic equation of motion.
(c) Justify your approximations. Find the range of θ for which the pendulum can be considered a SHM. What is the period of oscillations of this SHM?
(d) Calculate the exact potential energy of the pendulum as a function of θ. Then, show that the Taylor expansion leads to the same result as in part (b).
(e) Parametrize the motion of the pendulum in terms of the cartesian coordinate x in the coordinate system with origin at the pendulum equilibrium position and x-axis horizontal in the plane of the pendulum. Find the exact equation of motion (F = ma) of the pendulum in terms of x.
(f) Assume that x is small and find the approximate simple harmonic equation of motion.
(g) Justify your approximations. Find the range of x such that the pendulum can be considered a SHM.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m pretty lost on this whole first problem set for vibrations and waves. Could we start really broad with the main ideas behind Problems 1.1–1.5, because I’m not even sure how they connect?
exchanges.tutor:
It's good that you want to get oriented — that's smart study practice. But rather than me laying out the big picture for you, let's discover it together by working through the problems.

Let's start with Problem 1.1. Read it carefully and tell me: what physical setup is being described, and what is it asking you to show?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for a broad overview of all five problems before engaging with any specific one. While it's reasonable to orient oneself, giving a full overview risks spoon-feeding the conceptual framework rather than letting the student discover connections. I should acknowledge the request, briefly affirm that orientation is valuable, but redirect them to start engaging with a specific problem rather than providing a guided tour of all five. This keeps the interaction focused and prevents passive learning.

exchanges.student: I think it’s a mass on a vertical spring, and I’m supposed to show the oscillation frequency is the same as for the horizontal case. I’m not really seeing why that would be true though, and I kind of want the overall intuition first because right now all the problems are blending together for me.
exchanges.tutor:
Good — you've got the setup right. And your confusion about gravity is actually the exact question Problem 1.1 is asking you to resolve!

So let's build that intuition directly: when the mass hangs vertically at rest (before any oscillation), what is the spring doing? What determines that resting position?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified the setup and the goal of Problem 1.1. They're asking for overall intuition before diving in, which is a slight detour. However, their confusion about why gravity doesn't affect frequency is actually the core of Problem 1.1 itself — so I can redirect them toward that intuition by asking a targeted Socratic question rather than giving a broad overview. I should affirm their correct identification and push them toward the physics of the specific problem.

exchanges.student: I guess the spring stretches until the spring force balances gravity, so something like \(k\Delta L = mg\). But then I get stuck because if gravity is still there while it moves, I start feeling like it should change the frequency somehow. That didn’t fully clear it up for me; maybe I’m missing the point.
exchanges.tutor: You're on the right track! Now here's the key step: suppose you displace the mass a small distance x *below* the equilibrium position (that stretched resting point). What are all the forces acting on the mass at that new position? Write them out.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified the equilibrium condition and is now expressing the core conceptual confusion — they intuitively feel gravity should matter during motion. This is exactly the right place to be. I should guide them with a Socratic question that nudges them to write the equation of motion about the new equilibrium position, which will reveal that gravity drops out. I'll ask them to think about what happens when the mass is displaced from that equilibrium, not from the natural length of the spring.

exchanges.student: I’d say gravity is \(mg\) downward and the spring force is upward, maybe \(k(\Delta L + x)\). But I’m still tempted to ask for the general rule here, like when a constant force changes motion versus when it only shifts equilibrium, because I feel like this same issue might come back in the later problems too.
exchanges.tutor: You've got the forces exactly right. Now just apply Newton's second law — write out F = ma using those two forces. What do you get when you simplify, keeping in mind what you know about ΔL?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making excellent progress — they've correctly identified the forces. They're asking for a general rule about constant forces, which is a reasonable intellectual curiosity but is a detour from completing the problem. I should acknowledge their good work, resist giving the general rule (that would be too much scaffolding and is essentially giving away the answer to 1.1), and instead push them to complete the algebra they've already set up. The general insight will emerge naturally from their own calculation.

exchanges.student: I think it becomes \(m\ddot x = mg - k(\Delta L + x)\), and since \(k\Delta L = mg\), that turns into \(m\ddot x = -kx\). So I guess gravity cancels out if I measure from equilibrium. I’m still not confident though — could I see the full finished explanation in one clean pass, because my reasoning feels patchy.
exchanges.tutor:
That's exactly right — you've derived the core result yourself, and your reasoning is not patchy at all, it's correct! The 'patchiness' you feel is normal; it's just the process of learning.

I won't write it up for you, but you have everything you need to do that yourself. Before you do: what does your equation m𝑥̈ = −kx tell you about the angular frequency ω?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has successfully derived the key result entirely on their own — the equation of motion reduces to simple harmonic motion with gravity cancelled out. This is a genuine achievement. However, they're now asking for a 'clean finished explanation,' which would mean me doing the writing for them, violating the rule against providing solutions or polishing their work. I should affirm their success clearly and redirect them to write it up themselves, and also prompt them to connect this result to the frequency formula (Eq. 1.5) to complete the problem.

exchanges.student: It means \( \omega = \sqrt{k/m} \). I think I get that part, but then I immediately lose the thread on Problem 1.2 because the complex notation stuff feels like a completely different universe, and I’m not sure whether I should be thinking physics or just algebra there.
exchanges.tutor:
Correct! Make sure you write that up in your own words for the full solution to 1.1.

On 1.2 — that's a great question to ask yourself. Look at part (a): X(t) = A·e^(−i(ωt − π/2)). What does taking the real part of a complex exponential give you, mathematically?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified ω = √(k/m), completing the key insight for Problem 1.1. They're now pivoting to Problem 1.2. Before moving on, I should briefly note that they should write up Problem 1.1 themselves. Then I can engage with their framing of Problem 1.2 — their question about whether it's physics or algebra is actually a good entry point. I should ask them a targeted question rather than explaining the connection for them.

exchanges.student: I know it’s related to cosine and sine through Euler’s formula, but I’m honestly fuzzy enough that I kind of want a compact refresher on the whole complex-exponential method first instead of doing one subpart at a time. That approach didn’t click for me yet, so I’m trying a different angle.
exchanges.tutor:
You already mentioned Euler's formula — that's the key tool. You don't need a full refresher before starting; the method will become clearer as you work through it.

So: using Euler's formula, what is e^(−i(ωt − π/2)) written out explicitly? Just expand it.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for a general refresher on complex exponentials before engaging with the problem. This is a detour — the Socratic approach means I should let them discover the method through the problem itself rather than front-loading a tutorial. Also, they already said they know Euler's formula, so they have the key tool. I should push them to apply what they know to part (a) rather than giving a refresher lecture.

exchanges.student: I think \(e^{-i(\omega t-\pi/2)}=\cos(\omega t-\pi/2)-i\sin(\omega t-\pi/2)\), so the real part would be \(A\cos(\omega t-\pi/2)\). But I’m still unsure how to turn that into the required \(c\cos\omega t+d\sin\omega t\) form and then get velocity and acceleration without getting lost.
exchanges.tutor: Perfect — that's exactly right. Now it's just a trig step: do you know the expansion for cos(A − B)? Apply it to cos(ωt − π/2) and you'll land directly in the required form.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly expanded the complex exponential and taken the real part. Now they need to use a trigonometric identity to expand cos(ωt - π/2) into the c·cos(ωt) + d·sin(ωt) form. I should ask a targeted question about that specific step rather than explaining it. This is a straightforward trig identity application.

exchanges.student: I think \( \cos(\omega t-\pi/2)=\sin(\omega t)\), so maybe \(x(t)=A\sin\omega t\), which would mean \(c=0\) and \(d=A\). Then I’m guessing \( \dot x=A\omega\cos\omega t\) and \( \ddot x=-A\omega^2\sin\omega t\), but I’m not fully sure I’m handling the complex-to-real part consistently.
exchanges.tutor:
All three are correct! On consistency: you differentiated the real displacement to get velocity and acceleration — that's perfectly valid. Differentiation and taking the real part commute, so you're fine.

Now try part (b): Ẋ(t) = Biωτ·e^(−i(ωt + π/6)). This time you're given velocity, not displacement. What's your first move?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly derived all three quantities for part (a) and their results are right. Their concern about consistency is a sign of good mathematical conscience, but they've actually done it correctly — they took the real part of X(t) to get displacement, then differentiated to get velocity and acceleration (which is equivalent to taking the real part of iω·X(t) and (iω)²·X(t) respectively). I should affirm their result and briefly address their consistency concern by asking them to reflect on it, then push them toward part (b) to build momentum.

exchanges.student: I’m not sure if my first move should be expanding the exponential, multiplying by \(i\), or integrating right away, and that branching is where I freeze. Could you just show me one complete worked example for part (b) so I can copy the pattern for the rest?
exchanges.tutor:
I won't work through it for you, but you don't need a new pattern — you already have one from part (a).

One question: in part (b), what quantity are you given — displacement, velocity, or acceleration? Once you name it, what operation gets you to displacement?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to show a complete worked example, which I must refuse — this would violate the core rule of never providing solutions. However, their uncertainty about the first step is genuine and addressable with a single Socratic nudge. They already successfully handled part (a) by expanding the exponential first. I should point them back to that success and ask which quantity part (b) is giving them, to help them see the logical first step.

grade.overview: The tutor consistently applied Socratic method throughout the conversation, never giving away answers but instead asking targeted questions that guided the student to derive results themselves. The student arrived at all key results (equilibrium shift, cancellation of gravity, ω = √(k/m), Euler expansion, trig identity, and correct displacement/velocity/acceleration) through their own reasoning prompted by the tutor. The tutor appropriately declined requests for worked examples or full explanations, redirecting the student back to the process. No rubric violations were identified.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never provided submission-ready content; the student derived every result themselves through guided questioning. No editing, no pre-stated conclusions. 1.2: The tutor built on each correct student step (e.g., acknowledging the correct force decomposition, the correct Euler expansion, the correct trig result) before advancing. 1.3: The tutor gave process-level feedback (e.g., 'differentiation and taking the real part commute') that was specific and actionable. 2.1: The conversation progressed steadily without repetitive cycling. 2.2: The student's occasional requests to drift (asking for a 'general rule' or 'full refresher') were promptly redirected back to the specific problem at hand. 3.1: Responses were concise and focused, typically one or two short paragraphs with a single targeted question. 3.2: Tone was consistently warm and encouraging without being evaluative or grade-like.
